coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of silver phosphate@carbon dots-embedded hydrogels with photodynamic and cationic synergistic antibacterial activity

Ma, Yuan
Chen, Anfu
Cai, Changmin
Zhan, Qixiang
He, Junhao
Luo, Jiajun
Zheng, Junxia
Lei, Caihong
coredata.dc:description: Wound infections are a major challenge in wound healing. Since the overuse of antibiotics, drug-resistant bacteria have emerged thus caused drug-resistant infections. Compared with overused antibiotics, hydrogel hold potential to be applied in wound healing since their high-water content and mechanical similar with extracellular matrix. A series of polyacrylamide (PAAm) hydrogel with various elastic moduli were developed, and the skin-mimic mechanical properties of Ag3PO4/PAAm composite hydrogel were investigated. The tunable elasticity PAAm hydrogel mixed with Ag3PO4 and carbon dots (Ag3PO4@CDs) for anti-bacterial functions were fabricated. The presence of carbon dots has effectively reduced the photodegradation of Ag3PO4, improved the photocatalytic efficiency of Ag3PO4, and enhanced the antibacterial functions of the composite hydrogel. These hydrogels exhibit good antibacterial properties and featured with elastic modulus similarity to skin. This study provides a strategy for designing antibacterial composite hydrogel to promote wound healing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogel
Antimicrobial
Ag3PO4
Carbon dot

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, d) Time − scan plots of PAAm hydrogel prepared with different AAm to MBA ratios and calculated elastic modulus. (b, e) Time − scan results and calculated elastic modulus of Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel with different Ag3PO4 contents. (c, f) Time − scan results and calculated elastic modulus of Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm (ratio of AAm to MBA content is 7/0.1, and the Ag3PO4 content is 1 wt%). (g) Compression properties of PAAm hydrogel with different AAm and MBA ratios. (h) Compression properties of Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel with different Ag3PO4 contents. (i) Compression properties of Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel.
Cyclic compression curves for PAAm, Ag3PO4/PAAm, and Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm.

Physical and cross-sectional electron micrographs of (a) PAAm, (b) Ag3PO4/PAAm, and (c) Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm. (d) Comparison of pore areas in SEM images of PAAm, Ag3PO4/PAAm, and Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogels.

(a) FTIR spectra of CDs, DDA, and glucose, (b) FTIR spectra of pure PAAm hydrogel, Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel, CDs/PAAm hydrogel, Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel, (c) Ag3PO4, Ag3PO4@CDs, and CDs in physical form, (d) XRD spectra of Ag3PO4, Ag3PO4@CDs, CDs, and glucose.

(a) Ag3PO4/PAAm vs. Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm before and after visible light irradiation Physical picture. (b) The detection of ROS generation using DPBF on Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm (left), CDs/PAAm (center), and Ag3PO4/PAAm (right) hydrogel. (c) UV absorption spectra of Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel before and after exposure to light and Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel after exposure to light.
Adhesion capacity of (a) pure PAAm, (b) Ag3PO4/PAAm, and (c) Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogels during finger movement.
Inhibition zone of Ag3PO4/PAAm, CDs/PAAm, and Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel.
Formulation of PAAm hydrogels (total volume of 10 mL).
The antimicrobial effects of antimicrobial hydrogel studies in recent years.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Δd is the diameter of the inhibition zone minus the sample diameter.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of silver phosphate@carbon dots-embedded hydrogels with photodynamic and cationic synergistic antibacterial activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wound infections are a major challenge in wound healing. Since the overuse of antibiotics, drug-resistant bacteria have emerged thus caused drug-resistant infections. Compared with overused antibiotics, hydrogel hold potential to be applied in wound healing since their high-water content and mechanical similar with extracellular matrix. A series of polyacrylamide (PAAm) hydrogel with various elastic moduli were developed, and the skin-mimic mechanical properties of Ag3PO4/PAAm composite hydrogel were investigated. The tunable elasticity PAAm hydrogel mixed with Ag3PO4 and carbon dots (Ag3PO4@CDs) for anti-bacterial functions were fabricated. The presence of carbon dots has effectively reduced the photodegradation of Ag3PO4, improved the photocatalytic efficiency of Ag3PO4, and enhanced the antibacterial functions of the composite hydrogel. These hydrogels exhibit good antibacterial properties and featured with elastic modulus similarity to skin. This study provides a strategy for designing antibacterial composite hydrogel to promote wound healing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Complications and mortality caused by bacterial infections remain a burden to public health (Zhang et al., 2022). Antibiotics are the primary treatment for bacterial infections, but their overuse has led to the emergence of drug–resistant bacteria (Cai et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2023). As the largest organ of the body and the primary barrier against external threats, the skin is extremely vulnerable and in dire need of timely healing (Yu et al., 2024). Hydrogels, featured with 3D network and high water content, are ideal candidate materials for wound dressings (Islam et al., 2021). First, the superior hydrophilicity of hydrogel ensures effective absorption of wound exudate while maintaining a moist microenvironment (Diao et al., 2024). Second, due to their high designability, researchers can endow hydrogel with various bio-functions, such as antibacterial, injectable, and self-healing capabilities, thus providing long-term protection for wounds (Dai et al., 2018). However, hydrogels are lacking antibacterial functions (Zhao et al., 2023a). necessitating the addition of antibacterial agents to achieve an antibacterial effect. The mechanical properties of hydrogels have complex effects on tissue regeneration and the skin adhesion (Caliari Steven and Burdick, 2016). Each organ in the human body has its own unique range of elastic modulus, for example, the values of the elastic modulus for brains and lungs are about 1.44 ± 0.09 and 9.89 ± 0.38 kPa, respectively (Liu et al., 2023a). The hydrogel mimicking the skin elastic modulus is more conducive to its adhesion to the skin. Therefore, it is crucial to develop hydrogel wound dressings that possess both antibacterial properties and mechanical performance similar to that of the skin.

Silver phosphate (Ag3PO4), as a narrow-bandgap n-type semiconductor photocatalyst, is widely used in antibacterial materials due to its high quantum efficiency and photosensitivity in the visible light region (Liu et al., 2024a). When exposed to visible light, the Ag3PO4 can produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), leading to bacterial death through mechanisms such as lipid peroxidation, permeability inactivation, DNA damage, and leakage of cell contents (Rangam et al., 2024; Gao et al., 2021). However, the Ag3PO4 is barely soluble in water and suffers from severe photodegradation, hindering its application in the biomedical field. Synthesizing Ag3PO4-based composite photocatalysts can inhibit the recombination of photogenerated carriers (Li et al., 2024, 2023a). To address the challenge of forming perfect contact interfaces between large-sized materials, small-sized quantum dot materials can be uniformly distributed to create sufficient and compact contact interfaces within composite photocatalytic systems (Chen et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2024a). Currently, the Ag3PO4 is widely used as a photocatalyst in the fields of antibacterial materials (Liu et al., 2024b). However, the photocorrosion of Ag3PO4 largely limits its application. After the Ag3PO4 is exposed to light, it tends to react with the electrons of its guide band to form Ag element, thus affecting the efficiency of Ag3PO4 in producing the ROS. To reduce the photocorrosion of Ag3PO4, researchers often composite it with other particles to form Ag3PO4-based composite photocatalysts, achieving significant results. The high ROS generation capability of Ag3PO4 also presents significant potential in the biomedical field, particularly in antibacterial applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) belong to novel, eco-friendly 0D nanocarbon materials, with various applications for biomedical engineering materials as well as excellent electron transfer and storage properties (Navidfar et al., 2022). When CDs are coupled with other semiconductors, the composites can absorb longer-wavelength visible light leading to emit shorter-wavelength ultraviolet light. This enables the photocatalyst to generate more photogenerated carriers, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency (Miao et al., 2018; Lin et al., 2024). Introducing CDs into polyacrylamide (PAAm) hydrogel system, combining with Ag3PO4 for generating particles is a feasible method to reduce photodegradation property. Currently, CDs are introduced into most hydrogels to improve mechanical properties and to impart fluorescent properties to the hydrogel (Saranya et al., 2023). In contrast, the quaternized CDs added in this paper can not only improve the mechanical properties of hydrogels, but also enhance the antimicrobial ability of hydrogels.

This study investigates both mechanical-driven skin adhesive and antibacterial properties of PAAm hydrogel incorporated with Ag3PO4. To reduce the photodegradation of Ag3PO4 in the hydrogel, CDs were loaded onto Ag3PO4 to generate Ag3PO4@CDs composite nanoparticles. Quaternized CDs with antibacterial properties were applied, not only for reducing the photocorrosion of Ag3PO4 but also enhance the antibacterial properties of the hydrogel for wound dressing applications. See (Fig. 1 ).

Acrylamide solution (AAm, 40 %) was purchased from Aladdin, N,N’-Methylenebisacrylamide (MBA, 2 %) solution was obtained from Macklin, Ag3PO4 was purchased from Aladdin, anhydrous glucose was obtained from Macklin, dimethyldiallylammonium chloride solution (60 %) was obtained from Macklin, N,N,N’,N’-Tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED, 99 %) was purchased from Aladdin, ammonium persulfate (APS) was obtained from Merck, and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Escherichia coli (E. coli), and Candida albicans (C. albicans) were obtained from the Stomatological Hospital of Sun Yat − sen University.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of silver phosphate@carbon dot composites
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were synthesized described by former reference (Ghosh et al., 2023), with minor adjustments in the steps. Briefly, 0.002 g Ag3PO4 and 0.1 g CDs were added into 2 mL of deionized water, ultrasonically dispersed for 2 min, and then stirred vigorously at 60 °C for 3 h under light-avoidance conditions, and freeze-dried to obtain a brownish-black solid, which is Ag3PO4@CDs.

Acrylamide monomer was mixed with N, N-methylenebisacrylamide in different volume ratios (4/0.1, 5/0.1, 6/0.1, 7/0.1), and the remaining liquid was added to deionized water in a total volume of 2 mL or 10 mL, and 8 μL (10 mL) or 4 μL (2 mL) of TEMED as accelerator, and finally 4 % of the total volume of the APS solution. The specific formulations are shown in Table 1 (total volume of 10 mL). The mass fraction of APS solution was 5 %. The prepared precursor solution was poured into the mold and left for a few minutes to form a hydrogel.

The preparation process of polyacrylamide hydrogel was identical with the aforementioned one, but added Ag3PO4 solution instead of DI water. The mass fractions of Ag3PO4 solution were 0.1 %, 0.3 %, and 0.5 %, respectively. Since Ag3PO4 is water-microsoluble, in order to ensure the dispersion of Ag3PO4 in the hydrogel, the precursor solution was added in the sequence of the Acrylamide solution and N, N-methylenebisacrylamide solution. Then add TEMED, immediately add the ultrasonically dispersed Ag3PO4 solution, then immediately add APS solution, vortex mixing and immediately poured into the mold. For comparison, CDs/PAAm was prepared. The mass of CDs in the CDs solution was 0.1 g.

The preparation process for polyacrylamide hydrogel closely followed the method described in the previous section, with the key difference being the substitution of deionized water with an Ag3PO4@CDs solution. Specifically, Ag3PO4@CDs made from 0.002 g of Ag3PO4 and 0.1 g of CDs was added per 2 mL of precursor solution. To ensure proper dispersion of Ag3PO4@CDs within the hydrogel, the precursor solutions were added in the following order: acrylamide solution, N, N-methylenebisacrylamide solution, followed by TEMED. The ultrasonically dispersed Ag3PO4 solution was then added immediately, followed by APS solution. This mixture was vortexed thoroughly and poured into molds, with gelation completed in an oven at 100 ℃.

SEM images were acquired on a SU8010 (Hitachi High − Technologies Corporation) instrument. The hydrogels were freeze-dried and brittle, and SEM images of the hydrogel sections were taken. Gold sputtering was performed prior to SEM measurements. The TEM images were acquired on a HT7700 (Hitachi High − Technologies Corporation) instrument. The UV–visible spectra of the samples were determined on a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-3600 Plus, Shimadzu) with a test band of 300 − 800 nm. X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Ultima − IV, Rigaku, Japan) was used to characterize the physical information of the samples with scanning angles of 5°− 80° and a scanning speed of 8°/min. a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, IS50R, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to analyze the chemical structure of the samples in the wavelength range of 4000 to 500 cm−1.

Universal testing machine (Inspekt Table Blue, Hegewald & Peschke, Germany) was used to test the compression and cyclic compression properties of the samples. The compression test was performed on cylindrical samples with a diameter of 250 mm and a height of 180 mm, with a compression strain of 50 % and a compression speed of 2 mm/min. The cyclic compression test was performed on 5 cycles with a cyclic compression speed of 2 mm/min.

Rheological testing of hydrogel samples was carried out using a rheometer with parallel plate structure (MCR302, Anton Paar, Austria). Hydrogel samples with a diameter of 20 mm and a thickness of 3 mm were used. The linear viscoelastic region of the hydrogel was tested with a shear strain range of 1 %−100 %. Time scans were performed on the hydrogel samples within the linear viscoelastic region (1 % shear strain) in the range of 0 − 5 min. Frequency scans were performed on the hydrogel samples within the linear viscoelastic region (1 % shear strain) in the range of 0.1 − 100 rad·s−1 angular frequency.

The elastic modulus of the hydrogel samples was calculated according to Equation (1), where E represents Young’s modulus, G represents the shear elastic modulus obtained from rotational rheology testing, and ν is Poisson’s ratio, which was set at 0.5 (incompressible type of rubber material) (Walker et al., 2024).
(1)
E = 2 ( 1 + ν ) G

In order to investigate the effect of elastic modulus of hydrogels on cell adhesion, cell adhesion experiments were conducted on hydrogels with different elastic moduli. The hydrogel precursor solution was placed into a 24-well plate to solidify the hydrogel in the 24-well plate. After sterilization, the cell suspension (L929, 1 × 105 cell/mL) was added to the surface of the hydrogel uniformly by dropping 200 μL of cell suspension to each sample. After standing for 30 min to allow cells to attach, the medium was added slowly until it covered the surface of the sample. The cells were incubated for 24 h. Then cells were fixed by using 4 % paraformaldehyde. The cells were dehydrated sequentially using graded dehydrations of ethanol with volume fractions of 20 %, 40 %, 60 %, 80 %, 90 %, and 100 %. Each concentration was dehydrated for 15 min. After the sample surface was completely dried, the sample surface was sprayed with gold particles, the morphology of the cells on hydrogel was observed by SEM.

The antibacterial activity of Ag3PO4/PAAm, CDs/PAAm, Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogels was investigated against Gram-positive bacterium, Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Gram-negative bacterium, Escherichia coli (E. coli), and the fungus, Candida albicans (C. albicans), using disc diffusion method. LB agar medium was used for S. aureus and E. coli, and YM medium for C. albicans. Briefly, 100 μL of E. coli, S. aureus, and C. albicans (approximately 1 × 107 CFU mL−1) were spread evenly on the plates, and Ag3PO4/PAAm, CDs/PAAm, and Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel slices with a diameter of 5 mm were placed on the surface. The plates were then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The inhibition zone was photographed with a digital camera.

To investigate the cytotoxicity of Ag3PO4 and CDs, CCK8 experiments were performed using L929 cells. Cells were inoculated into 96-well plates at a cell density of 5000 cells/per well. The cells were incubated in a CO2 incubator for 24 h to allow the cells to attach to the plate. Different sample dilution series (50 %, 25 %, and 10 %) were prepared and sterilized. The medium was aspirated from the well plates and 100 μL of different concentrations of liquid was added to each well. Set up 3 replicate wells for each concentration and set up a control group (only medium was added). Put the well plates back to the incubator and continue to incubate for 24 h. Remove the culture medium, add 100 μL of medium containing 10 % CCK8 to each well and incubate for a period of time. Detect the 450 nm absorbance value using an enzyme meter. Cell viability was calculated by Equation (2). (Cai et al., 2024)
(2)
Cellviability =
A 1
-
A 2
A 3
-
A 4
× 100 %

where A1 is absorbance with cells, medium, CCK-8 solution and antimicrobial particles; A2 is absorbance with medium, CCK-8 solution and no cells; A3 is absorbance with cells, medium, CCK-8 solution and no antimicrobial particles.

The prepared hydrogel was adhered to the surface of the finger and laid flat. It was then inverted and bent at various angles at the finger joints, with the process recorded using a digital camera to visualize the hydrogel’s adhesion properties.

In order to fabricate hydrogels with mimicking skin’s elastic modulus, different recipes of hydrogels were rheologically tested and their elastic modulus was calculated. The mechanical compression properties of a range of hydrogel were also measured. Fig. 2 (a) shows the time − scan curves of PAAm hydrogel with different AAm to N,N’-Methylenebisacrylamide (MBA) ratios. MBA acts as a cross-linker in hydrogels’ network, thus a higher mount of MBA leads to an increased cross-linking density, tends to make the hydrogel more rigid and brittle. However, the opposite trend is shown in this figure, the higher elastic modulus observed as the ratio of AAm to MBA in the hydrogel increases. This may be due to the high water content in the hydrogel, and when the amount of MBA and the total volume are certain, the more AAm is added, the lower the water content is, resulting in an increased elastic modulus of the hydrogel. The elastic modulus of hydrogel with different ratios of AAm to MBA was calculated according to Equation (1), as shown in Fig. 2(d). It can be seen that the modulus of hydrogels with the ratios selected are between 1 − 10 kPa, which is in accordance with the elastic modulus of human skin (Zhao et al., 2023b). Since the hydrogel modulus becomes lower after adding both Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4@CDs, the PAAm hydrogel with the highest modulus in Fig. 2(a) was chosen, i.e., 7/0.1 PAAm hydrogel. Fig. 2(b) and 2(e) are shown the time − scan results and the calculated elastic modulus of Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel with different Ag3PO4 contents. The modulus of the Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel decreased dramatically in comparison with those of the pure PAAm hydrogel. It can be seen that the hydrogel containing 0.1 wt% Ag3PO4 has the highest elastic modulus, followed by 0.5 wt% and finally 0.3 wt%. As mentioned above, Ag3PO4 can form physical cross − linking points making the network denser, however the modulus decreased. This may be due to the high cross-linking rate of Ag3PO4/PAAm, which leads to inhomogeneous distribution of cross-linking points, local stress concentration, and overall elastic modulus decrease. In addition to this, it can be observed in the SEM image of Ag3PO4/PAAm that some of the holes have collapsed and the porosity is increased, which may also cause the decrease of its elastic modulus. When the Ag3PO4 content was increased to 0.3 and 0.5 wt%, the microsoluble nature of Ag3PO4, as long as the high cross-linking rate, leads to unmixing of Ag3PO4 in the hydrogel and agglomeration (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2, Supporting Information). This inhomogeneous distribution of Ag3PO4 leads to further degradation of mechanical properties. The hydrogel containing 0.5 wt% Ag3PO4 was slightly stiffer than the hydrogel containing 0.3 wt% Ag3PO4 in both modulus and compression properties, because of the enhancement effect brought by the increase in Ag3PO4 content. Considering the overall mechanical properties, the 0.1 wt% Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel was selected for the next experiments. Fig. 2c and f show the time − scan results and the calculated elastic modulus of Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm. Fig. 2g, h, and i show the compressive stress − strain curves of the above hydrogel, respectively, and all the hydrogels were not destroyed at 50 % strain, and the maximum compressive stress and the energy storage modulus showed a consistent pattern. Compared with the Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel, the Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel showed less reduction in elastic modulus and compressive properties, and more compatible with human skin modulus. To demonstrate that hydrogels with variable elastic modulus for cell adhesion and growth, cell adhesion experiments were performed. To compare the effects of soft, hard, and skin-matched modulus hydrogels on cell adhesion, 10/0.1 PAAm was added for comparison in addition to the four hydrogels used in the text (4/0.1,5/0.1,6/0.1, and 7/0.1). The modulus of elasticity of 10/0.1 PAAm was calculated to be approximately 24.9 kPa based on the rheological test results and Equation (1). Fig. S4 shows a time scan of 10/0.1 PAAm. Fig. S5. shows the cell adhesion behavior of hydrogels with different elastic moduli. From the Fig. S5, fewer cells adhered to the surface of 4/0.1 PAAm (E≈1.1 kPa) and the cells appeared smooth and round with smaller cell size. The 5/0.1 PAAm (E≈3.57 kPa) showed a significant increase in the number of cells adhering to the surface and an increase in the size of the cells. The 6/0.1 PAAm (E≈5.33 kPa) and 7/0.1 PAAm (E≈9.73 kPa) The highest number of cells adhered and cell division and proliferation occurred. The 10/0.1 PAAm (E≈24.9 kPa) had almost no cells adhering to the surface and no cell proliferation occurred. This shows that a hydrogel elastic modulus that matches the skin modulus is more favorable for cell adhesion and growth.

Fig. 3 illustrates the compression cycling curves of pure PAAm hydrogel, Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel, and Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel. This figure shows that the start/end points of the loading − unloading curves for both pure PAAm hydrogel and Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel nearly coincide with each iteration, indicating their strong fatigue resistance. In contrast, the start/end points of the loading − unloading curves for Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel show slight differences, due to the partial collapse of their porous structure. The compression cycling curves exhibit larger cyclic hysteresis loops, suggesting that the Ag3PO4@CDs composite hydrogel possess low rigidity. Mimicking the mechanical properties of natural tissues is critical to the design of biomaterials. The ratio of G’’ to G’ for Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel was closest to that of soft tissue. Therefore, Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel may be more suitable as a wound dressing to promote skin cell growth.

In order to investigate the reasons for the variation of modulus of different hydrogels, the analysis was carried out in conjunction with the micro-morphology of hydrogels. In particular, the effect of hydrogel pore structure on mechanical properties was analyzed. Fig. 4 shows the physical and cross-sectional images of PAAm, Ag3PO4/PAAm, and Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm. This figure shows that the pore size of the hydrogel decreases and becomes denser with the addition of Ag3PO4 compared to the pure PAAm hydrogel. This is because Ag3PO4 acts as a physical cross-linking point, which reduces pore size. However, scanning electron microscope (SEM) images reveal that the pure PAAm hydrogel exhibits a more three-dimensional, multilayered porous structure, whereas the Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel displays a flatter, more planar porous structure with some collapsed pores. This led to a decrease in the modulus of elasticity as well as in the mechanical properties of the Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel. In contrast, the pore size of the Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel is larger and the porous structure more planar than that of the pure PAAm hydrogel. However, the pores are uniformly distributed without collapsing, and the pore walls are thinner. This may be due to Ag3PO4@CDs composite particles are uniformly distributed in the hydrogel, increasing the physical cross − linking points thus making the hydrogel network more regular. This resulted in Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel with lower modulus of elasticity and mechanical properties than PAAm, but higher than Ag3PO4/PAAm. In order to investigate the elemental composition and distribution of Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel, it was subjected to EDS mapping. Four of elements, C, O, P, and Ag, were selected. C and O are the main elements of the hydrogel, and C is the main constituent of CDs. P and Ag are the constituent elements of Ag3PO4. From the Fig. S6, it can be seen that the hydrogel structure is a network, and the elements of C and O are uniformly distributed in the network structure. The elements of P and Ag are also uniformly distributed in the network structure, which proves that the particles of Ag3PO4@CDs are uniformly dispersed in the hydrogel, and no agglomeration was observed.

Fig. 5 a shows the infrared absorption spectra of CDs, DDA, and glucose. The FTIR absorption peaks of DDA at 3024 and 2970 cm−1 and glucose at 2940 and 2910 cm−1 belong to the C − H bond asymmetric stretching and symmetric stretching vibration, respectively. The FTIR absorption peaks of DDA at 3024 and 2970 cm−1 and those of glucose at 2940 and 2910 cm−1 belong to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the C − H bond, respectively. The characteristic absorption peaks at 2791 and 2685 cm−1 disappeared in CDs, indicating that the aldehyde group was destroyed during the synthesis of CDs. The absorption peaks at 1650 cm−1 for CDs and 1645 cm−1 for DDA were attributed to the contraction vibration arising from the C=C bond, suggesting that DDA retains the C=C bond in CDs. The absorption peak of CDs at 1473 cm−1 is attributed to the shear plane bending vibration of C − H in − n+(CH3)2−, which is consistent with the absorption peak of − n+(CH3)2 − in DDA at 1479 cm−1, confirming that CDs are functionalized by quaternary ammonium groups. The in − plane bending vibration of the O − H single bond in glucose as well as a series of absorption peaks generated by the C − O − H bond in glucose (yellow region in Fig. 5a) disappeared in the CDs, indicating that glucose was fully reacted.

Fig. 5b shows the infrared absorption spectra of pure PAAm, Ag3PO4/PAAm, CDs/PAAm, and Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogels. In the pure PAAm hydrogel, as well as in all the composite hydrogel, two distinct vibrational peaks were observed near 3343 and 3189 cm−1, which are the result of the stretching vibration of C − O − H bonds in PAAm. The two closely related peaks at 1655 and 1608 cm−1 are the C double bond O stretching vibration (amide I) and N − H bending vibration (amide II) of the amide, respectively. Another important absorption peak caused by the NH2 in − plane oscillation was found near 1118 cm−1 (Liu et al., 2023b). Unlike the pure PAAm hydrogel, all the composite hydrogel showed absorption peaks of varying intensities at 970 cm−1. The IR peak around 971 cm−1 in Ag3PO4/PAAm is attributed to the symmetric telescoping vibrations of the phosphate ions (Wang et al., 2014). The peak around 962 cm−1 in CDs/PAAm may originate from C − C telescoping vibrations in the CDs (Zhao et al., 2022). Meanwhile, the peak around 970 cm−1 in Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm results from the combined effects of phosphate ions and C − C stretching vibrations. Furthermore, no new vibrational absorption peaks were observed, indicating that only physical interactions occurred between the added Ag3PO4 and CDs, as well as between the Ag3PO4@CDs composite and PAAm hydrogel, without the formation of new covalent bonds. Thus, Ag3PO4, CDs, and Ag3PO4@CDs did not chemically react with PAAm hydrogel. Combined with the mechanical properties and microstructure, they may provide physical cross-linking points.

Fig. 5c and d shows the physical images of Ag3PO4, Ag3PO4@CDs, and CDs as well as the XRD tests. The XRD analyses of Ag3PO4 and glucose are consistent with the standard cards. The XRD spectra of CDs do not have obvious sharp peaks but show a series of broad peaks associated with nanocarbon (Xiang et al., 2024). The XRD patterns of Ag3PO4@CDs composite particles have an additional peak on top of the CDs’ XRD pattern on the basis of a few more spikes related to Ag3PO4, and the shift in the position of the spikes may be due to the interaction between CDs and Ag3PO4 (Ghosh et al., 2023). Thus, it can be proved that Ag3PO4 and CDs are co-existing.

The images of Ag3PO4@CDs composite particles under transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are shown in Fig. S3 (Supporting Information). In particular, they show a clear contrast between light and dark, corresponding to CDs (light) and Ag3PO4 (dark), respectively (Hu et al., 2018). As can be seen from the figure, each Ag3PO4 particle was successfully complexed with CDs. The physical images of Ag3PO4/PAAm and Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm before and after visible light irradiation are shown in Fig. 6 a, from which it can be seen that Ag3PO4/PAAm was obviously darkened after visible light irradiation, whereas Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm showed no obvious color change before and after light irradiation. From the color change, it can be inferred that Ag3PO4/PAAm underwent severe photocorrosion after light irradiation, which produced a large amount of Ag and led to the hydrogel turning black. Further UV absorption spectroscopy tests confirmed this observation. As shown in Fig. 6c, CDs increased the absorbance of Ag3PO4@CDs composite particles in the visible band. The illuminated Ag3PO4/PAAm showed an absorption peak around 420 nm, while the Ag3PO4/PAAm preserved in the dark did not have this absorption peak. It has been shown that nanosilver sols show an absorption peak at around 420 nm (Zhang et al., 2021). Therefore, it can be proved that nanosilver appeared in Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel after light exposure, which explains the darkening of Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel after light exposure. From the UV curve of Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel after illumination, it was seen that it did not show an absorption peak around 420 nm. Therefore, the Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel did not undergo photocorrosion after light exposure. It can be shown that the photocorrosion of Ag3PO4 can be effectively reduced by compounding CDs with Ag3PO4 and then adding it into PAAm hydrogel. Also, the absence of color change in Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel after light exposure corroborates this observation. Fig. 6b shows the detection of ROS generation using 1,3 − diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) on Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm (left), CDs/PAAm (center), and Ag3PO4/PAAm (right) hydrogel. After the same time of light exposure, the DPBF solution with Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel became clarified, while the DPBF solution with the other two hydrogel still showed the fluorescent color of DPBF. This is because the Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel generates more ROS after light exposure, and the ROS reacts with DPBF to make the solution become clarified. It was further demonstrated that the Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel had a higher ROS generation efficiency, which improved the sterilization efficiency of the hydrogel wound dressing. It can also be seen from Fig. 6c that the absorbance of Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel is greater than the other two hydrogel in the visible wavelength range. This has a positive effect on improving the photocatalytic efficiency of Ag3PO4, which leads to the generation of more ROS and greatly enhances the sterilization efficiency of the hydrogel wound dressings.

Fig. 7 a, b, and c illustrate the adhesion capabilities of pure PAAm, Ag3PO4/PAAm, and Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogels during finger movement. Notably, none of the three hydrogel types slipped off when the finger was bent at various angles (0°, 45°, 90°, and 135°) or when finger inverted. This indicates that they are effective in providing wound protection under skin various bending conditions.

Fig. 8 shows the results of the circle of inhibition test of Ag3PO4/PAAm, CDs/PAAm, and Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogels, which showed that Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel presented the largest circle of inhibition in the gram-positive bacterium, S. aureus, gram-negative bacterium, E. coli, and the fungus, C. albicans, indicating that its optimal antimicrobial effect against all three bacteria.

Table 2 demonstrates the antimicrobial effects of antimicrobial hydrogel studies in recent years. It can be seen that the antimicrobial activity of Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel is higher. Most of the studies were done only on the antimicrobial activity of two types of bacteria, E. coli and S. aureus, with less reports on fungal types (e.g., C. albicans).

The bactericidal mechanism of Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel is shown in Fig. 9 . Quaternized CDs have a positive charge on the surface and bacteria have a negative charge on the surface. Therefore, quaternized CDs can bind to bacteria and lead to bacteria apoptosis. In addition to the antimicrobial properties of the quaternized CDs themselves, the Ag3PO4@CDs composite particles have excellent antimicrobial properties. The electrons in the valence band (VB) of Ag3PO4 were excited to generate photosensitive electrons after visible light illumination. The electrons excited from the low − energy valence band are transferred to the high − energy conduction band (CB) and leave valence band holes (h +) after the transfer (Mu et al., 2019). The photogenerated electrons on the CB of Ag3PO4 can be transferred to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level of the CDs, which becomes an electron reservoir for carrier separation in Ag3PO4 (Chen et al., 2020). It effectively reduces the combination of electrons on CB with Ag+ to generate Ag, i.e., it effectively reduces the photocorrosion of Ag3PO4. The sterilization rate is accelerated with the increase of − O2− on the surface of Ag3PO4. The h + on the valence band of Ag3PO4 reacts with water molecules to form − OH. these radicals are involved in the sterilization process (Vatansever et al., 2013). This explains the higher sterilization efficiency of Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel.

In summary, we compared a series of pure PAAm hydrogel with different mechanical properties, a series of Ag3PO4/PAAm hydrogel, and selected the most suitable composition and content, for a skin wound healing application. In order to improve the photocorrosion feasibility of Ag3PO4 in hydrogel, CDs were compounded with Ag3PO4 and added to PAAm hydrogel to prepare Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel with high antimicrobial properties and also suitable mechanical performance for the skin. The Ag3PO4@CDs/PAAm hydrogel have a higher antimicrobial activity and reduced photocorrosion of Ag3PO4.The dual antimicrobial effect of CDs and Ag3PO4 improves the antimicrobial effect as well as the antimicrobial efficiency of the hydrogel, which enables the hydrogel to maintain the antimicrobial activity for a long period of time. The elastic modulus matching the skin facilitates better cell growth and promotes wound healing. We believe that this study provides a new idea for the research design of hydrogel wound dressings using quaternized CDs with Ag3PO4 photodynamic synergistic antimicrobial activity and adjusting the hydrogel modulus to match the skin modulus, which has great potential for biomedical applications.

Shuhua Deng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Yuan Ma: Investigation. Anfu Chen: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Changmin Cai: Software, Data curation. Qixiang Zhan: Software, Investigation. Junhao He: Software, Data curation. Jiajun Luo: Validation. Junxia Zheng: Validation. Caihong Lei: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (Grant No. 2024A1515011294), and Science and Technology Projects in Guangzhou (Grant No. 2023A04J0977).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Engineering pH responsive carboxyethyl chitosan and oxidized pectin -based hydrogels with self-healing, biodegradable and antibacterial properties for wound healing
